name: Albert Pike
description: Albert Pike (1809-1891) was a prominent figure in American history, serving as a Confederate general, lawyer, and writer. He is also known for his high-ranking position in Freemasonry, specifically the Scottish Rite. Conspiracy theories surrounding Pike often center on claims that he was a Satanist, a key architect of a New World Order, and that he predicted three World Wars in a letter to Giuseppe Mazzini. Proponents cite Pike's alleged involvement in the Ku Klux Klan and his writings on esoteric subjects as evidence. The most infamous claim involves a supposed letter outlining a plan for three world wars to establish a New World Order, with the third war pitting Zionists against Muslims. Mainstream historians and scholars largely dismiss these claims. The letter is widely considered a hoax, with no credible evidence supporting its existence or authenticity. The Grand Lodge of England has refuted Pike's alleged Satanism, and scholars point out that his writings on Freemasonry are often misinterpreted or taken out of context. Pike's actual views and activities are subject to ongoing historical debate, but the extreme claims attributed to him are generally regarded as unsubstantiated and based on misinformation and misinterpretations.
subtitle: Confederate General and Masonic Conspiracy Figure

claims.claim: Pike predicted three World Wars in a letter to Mazzini.
claims.evidence: The alleged 'Pike-Mazzini letter' detailing three world wars.
claims.criticism: The letter is widely regarded as a hoax. No original document exists, and its first known appearance was in the 1960s in works by conspiracy theorists. Mainstream historians dismiss it as fabricated.

claims.claim: Pike was a Satanist and Luciferian.
claims.evidence: Selective interpretations of Pike's writings on Freemasonry and esoteric subjects.
claims.criticism: The Grand Lodge of England denies Pike's alleged Satanism. Scholars argue that his writings are often taken out of context and misconstrued. His esoteric explorations do not necessarily equate to Satan worship.

claims.claim: Pike was a key architect of the New World Order.
claims.evidence: His high-ranking position in Freemasonry and alleged connections to secret societies.
claims.criticism: The concept of a 'New World Order' is itself a broad conspiracy theory. Pike's involvement in Freemasonry, while historically significant, does not provide evidence of a conspiracy to establish a global totalitarian regime.

keyFigures.name: Michael Haupt
keyFigures.role: Historian who investigated and debunked the Pike-Mazzini letter.

keyFigures.name: Fritz Springmeier
keyFigures.role: Conspiracy theorist promoting claims about Pike's satanism.

keyFigures.name: Arturo de Hoyos
keyFigures.role: Historian and Sovereign Grand Commander of the Scottish Rite, Southern Jurisdiction, who has written extensively on Pike's life and work.

mythologyConnections: Gnosticism (potential influences on Pike's esoteric thought)

timeline.event: Albert Pike born in Boston, Massachusetts.
Pike elected Sovereign Grand Commander of the Scottish Rite, Southern Jurisdiction.
Albert Pike dies in Washington, D.C.
The alleged 'Pike-Mazzini letter' first appears in conspiracy literature.

sources.author: Albert Pike
sources.work: Morals and Dogma of the Ancient and Accepted Scottish Rite of Freemasonry
sources.title: Albert Pike
